items.q: Were the USA and USSR natural friends who suddenly fell out after 1945?
items.correct: No - the Grand Alliance was a marriage of convenience against Hitler; ideological rivalry dated from 1917 and resurfaced as soon as Germany was beaten.
items.misconception: The superpowers were close allies with no tensions until the Cold War began without warning.
items.random_wrong: The USA and USSR were joined in a single federation during the war.
items.why: Seeing the deep pre-war distrust explains why wartime cooperation collapsed so quickly into the Cold War.

items.q: Did Gorbachev set out to destroy communism and the USSR?
items.correct: No - his reforms of glasnost and perestroika aimed to save the Soviet system, but they released criticism and national movements he could not control.
items.misconception: Gorbachev deliberately planned the break-up of the Soviet Union.
items.random_wrong: Gorbachev was an American politician sent to reform Russia.
items.why: Outcomes get read backwards as intentions; the gap between Gorbachev's aims and results is a classic exam discussion point.

items.q: Did the Cold War end because the USA defeated the USSR in battle?
items.correct: No - the superpowers never fought each other directly; the Cold War ended through Soviet economic failure, Gorbachev's reforms and the revolutions of 1989-91.
items.misconception: A final military victory by the USA ended the Cold War.
items.random_wrong: The Cold War ended when both sides destroyed all their weapons.
items.why: Students expect wars to end with battles; the Cold War's peaceful, internal collapse is exactly what made it distinctive.

items.q: What held the Grand Alliance together during the Second World War?
items.random_wrong: A joint commitment to free elections everywhere
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A common enemy - Nazi Germany. “A shared belief in communism” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What was Gorbachev's policy of glasnost?
items.correct: Greater openness, allowing freer speech and criticism in the USSR
items.misconception: The rapid privatisation of all Soviet industry
items.random_wrong: A programme to build more nuclear missiles
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Greater openness, allowing freer speech and criticism in the USSR. “The rapid privatisation of all Soviet industry” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which event in 1979 helped end detente?
items.random_wrong: The fall of the Berlin Wall
items.why: It's The Soviet invasion of Afghanistan. “The Cuban Missile Crisis” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Al-Qaeda terrorists attacked the World Trade Center and the Pentagon
items.why: Correct answer: Al-Qaeda terrorists attacked the World Trade Center and the Pentagon. “The USA invaded Iraq” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the Soviet Union dissolved?
items.why: Exact answer: 1991 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
